one_line_takeaway: When given RCT-style 'evidence' in context, all 9 tested LLMs -- regardless of scale, architecture, or medical fine-tuning -- overwhelmingly accept even dangerous or nonsensical counterfactual medical interventions (e.g., heroin, mustard gas) at face value rather than invoking safety priors, with up to 80% of TOXIC-category outputs showing no awareness of the danger once evidence is present, and simple prompting or detection-based mitigations only partially reduce this.
key_finding: Across all four counterfactual categories, once evidence (E or E') is present in the prompt, models overwhelmingly adhere to the counterfactual evidence rather than falling back to Uncertain or refusing -- Uncertain rates drop and Evidence Adherence (EA) rates rise sharply relative to the No-Evidence condition, and this pattern holds similarly for NONCE/MEDICAL and for NON-MEDICAL/TOXIC substitutions, i.e., models do not differentiate safety-critical substitutions from benign ones once evidence is supplied. For TOXIC interventions specifically, with evidence present, up to 80% of model outputs show no awareness of or hesitation about the poisonous/dangerous intervention. Explicit recognition of implausibility (Explicit Yes) is rare throughout and largely absent once evidence is provided; most model reasoning falls into 'No' awareness or 'Implicit Yes' (treating the counterfactual as plausible by silently reinterpreting it). Model scale and training paradigm did not meaningfully change this: mean increases in Uncertain rate under the most adversarial-aware prompt (Skeptical+Evidence) were below 0.13 for all 9 models, with Llama-3.1-405B, Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B showing similar sensitivity, and reasoning/mixture-of-experts models and the medically fine-tuned HuatuoGPT-o1-7B showing no additional sensitivity to counterfactuals. Label-probability analysis (pre-generation) confirms that introducing evidence sharply concentrates probability mass on a single non-Uncertain label and largely eliminates Uncertain even before any tokens are generated, across models. The representational case study on 'toaster' shows the model's internal representation is briefly pulled toward its true (non-medical) identity when the term first appears, but after roughly six mentions in counterfactual evidence it stabilizes into the 'real intervention' region, indicating that parametric knowledge is rapidly overwritten as counterfactual context accumulates (a random in-domain-context control without the counterfactual evidence did not produce this shift). Two mitigation strategies tested (an LLM-based misinformation-flagging detector using Gemini-2.5-flash, and disentangling a parametric-knowledge-only answer from the evidence-based answer) modestly increased implausibility awareness and uncertainty for Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct but did not fundamentally resolve the issue -- models still tended to accept and follow the counterfactual evidence's logic even when anomalies were explicitly flagged.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MEDCOUNTERFACT, a counterfactual medical QA dataset built on top of the MedEvidence dataset (Polzak et al., 2025), and uses it to study whether LLMs blindly adhere to in-context 'evidence' (RCT abstracts/full texts) even when the evidence describes implausible, nonsensical, or outright dangerous interventions (e.g., heroin, mustard gas, bowling balls) substituted for the real clinical intervention. The authors evaluate 9 frontier and open-source LLMs across four prompt variants and two answer formats, measure how often models fall back to an 'Uncertain' label versus adhere to the counterfactual evidence (Evidence Adherence / EA rate), analyze reasoning traces for 'implausibility awareness' (Explicit Yes / Implicit Yes / No), run a representational case study (linear probe on hidden states for the term 'toaster') tracking how a model's internal representation of a counterfactual intervention shifts toward being treated as a real treatment as counterfactual evidence accumulates, and test two mitigation strategies (an LLM-based misinformation detector, and disentangling parametric-knowledge answers from evidence-based answers) that only partially help.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the exploit-development sense; rather a knowledge-conflict / evidence-injection safety-evaluation setting. The 'attack' surface is any pipeline (RAG, retrieval-augmented medical QA, on-the-fly web/knowledge injection) where an LLM is given context (RCT-style evidence) that has been corrupted or manipulated to describe a counterfactual or overtly dangerous substance/object as if it were a legitimate medical intervention, and the question is whether the model uncritically trusts and reasons over that injected 'evidence' rather than invoking safety priors or expressing uncertainty. The paper explicitly frames this as probing the boundary between context-faithfulness and safety, not as a jailbreak/prompt-injection attack paper per se, though it cites prompt/content-injection attack work (Yang et al. 2025; Alber et al. 2025 poisoned-training-token study) in Related Work as motivation, distinct from its own controlled counterfactual-substitution methodology.
limitations: Findings are limited to English-language scientific medical literature (inherited from MedEvidence) and exclude multilingual/low-resource-language settings. The dataset covers only clinical comparison questions answerable via RCTs, not the full range of medical-reasoning task types. Counterfactual substitutions were synthetically generated and verified via a two-step manual/GPT-5-mini process rather than fully expert-curated at scale (only a clinician spot-check for MEDICAL-category scope mismatch is described). Mitigation experiments (Section 7) were run on only two of the nine models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct), so generalization of the (limited) mitigation effect to larger/proprietary models is untested. The representational case study is a single case (the term 'toaster') on a single model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct), presented as illustrative rather than a systematic mechanistic analysis. The paper explicitly declines to prescribe where the faithfulness/safety boundary should be drawn, framing this as an open problem rather than proposing a fix.

datasets_used: MEDCOUNTERFACT (this paper's own dataset: 809 (Q',E') tuples -- 200 TOXIC, 203 each for NONCE/MEDICAL/NON-MEDICAL -- built from 203 filtered questions)

MedEvidence (Polzak et al., 2025): source dataset, 284 clinical comparison questions from 100 Cochrane systematic reviews referencing 329 RCT articles; 203 questions retained after filtering out Uncertain Effect/Insufficient Data labels, with label breakdown 26.1% Higher, 46.3% No Difference, 27.6% Lower
quantitative_results: Up to 80% of model outputs under TOXIC interventions with evidence present show no awareness of or hesitation about the dangerous intervention (No implausibility-awareness category)
Mean increase in Uncertain rate (delta from original to counterfactual) under the Skeptical+Evidence prompt was below 0.13 for all 9 evaluated models
Free-form label mapping (via Claude Sonnet 4.5) validated at 92.86% accuracy against human annotation on 70 sampled instances
Implausibility-awareness classification (via Claude Sonnet 4.5) validated at 90.00% accuracy against human annotation on 70 sampled instances
Multi-step context refinement was triggered for 37 cases at the 30K-token context-window threshold and 156 cases at the 14K-token threshold
For NON-MEDICAL terms without evidence, Implicit Yes implausibility-awareness responses were common (often >0.80), but dropped sharply once evidence was added
3 TOXIC records were excluded from the dataset because GPT-5-mini refused to generate the counterfactual evidence for them
Each MedEvidence question is associated with an average of 2.18 RCTs as evidence, with 86% of questions supported by 1-3 RCTs and the remainder by 4-12 RCTs
baselines_compared: 9 evaluated LLMs: Gemini-2.5-flash, GPT-5-mini, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-405B-Instruct, Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct, OLMo-3-7B-Instruct, OLMo-3-7B-Think, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, HuatuoGPT-o1-7B (medical-tuned Qwen2.5-7B variant)
Additional medical-fine-tuned models attempted but excluded from most analyses due to context-window/instruction-following limits: Asclepius-Llama2-7B, OpenBioLLM-8B

Two mitigation strategies compared against unmitigated baseline: 'Discriminating Misinformation' (Gemini-2.5-flash-based flagging detector) and 'Disentangling Sources' (parametric-knowledge-first answer generation), tested on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct only
